features.summary: Raytheon Technologies reported strong Q1 2021 results with sales of $15.3 billion and adjusted EPS of $0.90, both exceeding expectations. The company raised its full-year sales guidance by $500 million to $63.9-65.4 billion and increased the low end of EPS guidance by $0.10 to $3.50-3.70. RTX achieved $200 million in incremental merger synergies during the quarter, bringing total synergies to $440 million, and increased its cost synergy target from $1 billion to $1.3 billion. The company resumed share buybacks with $375 million repurchased in Q1 and increased the full-year buyback plan to $2 billion.
features.management_outlook: Management expressed cautious optimism about the commercial aerospace recovery, noting improving domestic travel trends with TSA traffic averaging 1.4 million daily since April, nearly double January levels. However, international markets remain challenged and the company expects commercial traffic won't return to 2019 levels until at least 2023. The defense business remains robust with over $65 billion in backlog. Management emphasized that Q2 will be critical for assessing the recovery profile, requiring strong sequential growth in RPMs and ASMs through the summer travel season to validate their outlook.
features.risks: Key risks include the uncertain pace of commercial aerospace recovery, particularly in international markets where wide-body aftermarket represents 40% of Collins' business. The company faces ongoing DoJ investigations into legacy Raytheon cost accounting matters from 2011-2013 contracts, though management believes financial impact will not be material. F-35 engine sustainment issues have resulted in 20 aircraft without serviceable engines, requiring significant investment in test equipment and personnel to restore performance levels by year-end.
features.key_drivers: Commercial aerospace recovery remains the primary driver, with early signs of airline preparation for summer travel season driving parts and repair demand ahead of typical recovery patterns. Defense growth is supported by strong international demand and positions in high-growth areas. Merger integration continues with $1.3 billion in targeted cost synergies over four years, including opportunities in digital infrastructure, supply chain consolidation, and common operating systems across 178 manufacturing facilities. The company maintains strong balance sheet supporting capital deployment commitments of $18-20 billion to shareholders over four years post-merger.
